tools.title: hashcat
tools.purpose: The fastest GPU-accelerated hash cracker. Brute-forces passwords by wordlist, mask, or hybrid mode for hundreds of hash types. The main tool for offline password recovery.

flags.desc: Path to the wordlist (for -a 0/1 attacks) or the mask (for -a 3). Supplied last, after the hash file. Edit freely.

Hash type (--hash-type). Key ones: 0 MD5, 100 SHA1, 1000 NTLM, 1400 SHA256, 1800 sha512crypt, 3200 bcrypt, 5600 NetNTLMv2, 13100 Kerberos TGS-REP (Kerberoasting), 18200 Kerberos AS-REP (AS-REP Roasting), 16800 WPA-PMKID, 22000 WPA-PBKDF2.
Ignore usernames at the start of hash-file lines (user:hash format).
Show help for a specific -m: sample hash, salt type, etc.
Attack mode (--attack-mode): 0 straight wordlist, 1 combinator (2 wordlists), 3 mask brute-force, 6 hybrid wordlist+mask, 7 hybrid mask+wordlist.
Mask placeholders (for -a 3): ?l lowercase a-z, ?u uppercase A-Z, ?d digits 0-9, ?s special characters, ?a everything (?l?u?d?s), ?b byte 0x00-0xff. Example mask: Password?d?d.
Custom charset 1 (--custom-charset1..4: -1/-2/-3/-4), then used in the mask as ?1.
Increment the mask length (from short to long).
Minimum mask length with --increment.
Maximum mask length with --increment.
Word-mutation rules file (--rules-file). Repeatable. Classics: best64.rule, d3ad0ne.rule.
Optimized kernels (--optimized-kernel-enable): noticeably faster, but limits the maximum password length (usually 31).
Workload profile (--workload-profile): 1 low, 2 default, 3 high, 4 nightmare (headless only).
Device types (--opencl-device-types): 1 CPU, 2 GPU.
Ignore warnings (run without a supported GPU, etc.).
Show already cracked hashes from the potfile (compares the hashlist with the potfile) without cracking anything.
File to write found passwords to (--outfile).
Do not use or write the potfile (force a fresh crack).
Only generate candidates and print them without cracking (test masks/rules).
Session name for resuming (--session).
Resume an interrupted session (by the name from --session).
Abort after N seconds.

recipes.note: Wordlist attack on MD5 with rockyou - the baseline scenario.
NTLM hashes with a wordlist and best64 mutation rules.
WPA handshake: brute-force an 8-digit numeric password by mask.
Kerberoasting: crack TGS-REP tickets extracted from AD.
Show already cracked NTLM from the potfile without re-cracking.
Hybrid: a wordlist word + three digits at the end (sha512crypt).

tools.notes: The target is a hash file (one hash per line), followed by the wordlist or mask as a positional argument. The hash type (-m) is critical: the wrong mode = zero matches; determine it with hashid/--example-hashes. The potfile (~/.hashcat/hashcat.potfile) accumulates cracked hashes between runs. -O speeds things up but cuts the password length. On a VM/without a GPU you need --force. 22000 replaced the deprecated 2500/16800 for WPA.

tools.title: john (John the Ripper)
tools.purpose: The classic CPU password cracker with hash-type autodetection and a rich set of *2john converters. Handles ZIP/SSH/PDF/KeePass and hundreds of hash formats. Indispensable when there is no GPU.

flags.desc: Wordlist attack (--wordlist=FILE). Read candidates from a file.
Enable word-mutation rules (--rules[=SECTION]). Without a section it is [List.Rules:Wordlist].
Single crack mode: rules based on usernames/GECOS (--single). A fast first pass.
Incremental brute-force using a Markov model (--incremental[=MODE], default ASCII).
Mask attack (--mask=MASK). Placeholders as in hashcat: ?l ?u ?d ?s ?a.
Take candidates from john.pot (--loopback[=FILE]) - mutations of already cracked passwords.
Explicitly set the hash type (--format=NAME): NT, raw-md5, sha512crypt, bcrypt, krb5tgs, etc. Disables autodetection.
Show already cracked passwords for the file (--show[=left] - show the UNcracked ones).
Reference lists (--list=WHAT): formats, subformats, build-info, rules.
Benchmark formats for N seconds (--test[=TIME]).
Run N parallel processes on CPU cores (--fork=N).
Distributed cracking: range/total (--node=MIN[-MAX]/TOTAL).
Stop after N seconds.
Session name for resuming (--session=NAME, .rec file).
Resume an interrupted session (--restore[=NAME], default john.rec).
Show the status of an active/interrupted session (--status[=NAME]).
Custom potfile for results (--pot=NAME).
Only the specified users/UIDs (--users=[-]LOGIN|UID; minus = exclude).
Only print candidates without checking (--stdout[=LENGTH]) - a word generator.

recipes.note: Standard wordlist attack with format autodetection.
Wordlist + mutation rules (l33t, case, trailing digits).
Explicitly specify the NTLM format (autodetection on NT sometimes misses).
Show already cracked passwords from john.pot.
Extract the hash from a ZIP with the converter and crack it right away (same for ssh2john/pdf2john/keepass2john).
Full brute-force on 4 cores when wordlists did not help.

tools.notes: The target is a hash file (positional). The *2john converters (zip2john, ssh2john, rar2john, pdf2john, keepass2john, gpg2john, office2john) turn a protected file into a hash for john. Results accumulate in ~/.john/john.pot - a re-run does not re-crack what is already cracked (for a clean run use --pot or wipe the pot). Format names come from --list=formats. In Kali the binary is the Jumbo build (john-the-ripper-jumbo), hence the hundreds of formats.

tools.title: hashid / hash-identifier
tools.purpose: Identifies a hash type by its appearance and immediately suggests the hashcat mode (-m) and the John format (-j). The first step before any cracking: without the right type the cracker is useless.

flags.desc: Hash in quotes (positional). You can pass a path to a file with hashes or feed it via STDIN.
Show the matching hashcat mode (--mode), the number for the hashcat -m flag.
Show the matching John the Ripper format (--john), the name for --format.
Extended mode (--extended): enable all algorithms, including salted password hashes.
Write the result to a file instead of STDOUT (--outfile FILE).
Show help.

recipes.note: Basic identification: a list of likely hash types.
Show the hashcat mode and the John format at once - ready to copy into the cracker.
Extended list, including salted variants.
Identify the types for all hashes in a file.

tools.notes: hashid recognizes more types by structure/prefix than the old hash-identifier (especially with prefixes like $1$, $6$, $2y$). This is a heuristic: for short hashes (MD5/NTLM look identical - 32 hex) several candidates are returned, try them one by one. hash-identifier is an interactive analogue without flags (you type the hash at the prompt). An online/CLI alternative is name-that-hash (nth).

tools.title: cewl / crunch
tools.purpose: CeWL scrapes words from a target site into a custom wordlist (names, terms, products). Crunch generates a wordlist by mask/charset. Both produce targeted lists for a specific victim.

flags.desc: Spider crawl depth (--depth), default 2.
Minimum word length (--min_word_length), default 3.
Accept words that contain digits.
Convert all collected words to lowercase.
Show the frequency of each word (--count).
Allow the spider to go off to other sites (--offsite).
Collect metadata from files (--meta).
Collect email addresses (--email).
Separate file for metadata.
Separate file for email addresses.
Write the wordlist to a file (--write).
Custom User-Agent (--ua).
Verbose output (--verbose).

recipes.note: Collect words of 5+ letters from the page, depth 2, into a file.
Include words with digits (passwords like Company2024).
Also pull document metadata and email addresses.
Crunch: 4 lowercase letters + 4 digits (e.g. pass1234), save to a file.
Crunch: all 6-character hex combinations from the given charset.

tools.notes: CeWL: the target is a URL (positional), requires access to the site. The larger -d is, the wider the coverage and the longer it runs. The resulting list is fed into hashcat/john/hydra. CRUNCH (syntax: crunch MIN MAX [charset] [options]): -t pattern placeholders - @ lowercase letters, , (comma) uppercase letters, % digits, ^ symbols; the charset order is always lowercase-uppercase-digits-symbols, replace an unneeded set with a plus + (default charset). Crunch options: -o output file, -t pattern, -b max file size (1MB/1GB), -f charset.lst name (named charset), -p strings (permutations), -s start. Caution: crunch easily generates terabytes - estimate the size (the first line of output shows the size).

tools.title: ltrace / strace
tools.purpose: strace intercepts a program's system calls (open, read, connect), ltrace - library calls (strcmp, malloc). Dynamic binary analysis: you see files, network, and password comparisons in the clear.

flags.desc: Follow child processes (--follow-forks): fork/vfork/clone.
Call filter (--expression). Groups: trace=file, network, memory, signal, process; or a comma-separated list of names.
Which signals to show (minus/! = exclude).
Full structure output without abbreviation (--no-abbrev).
The traced binary with its arguments (positional, after the options): strace [options] ./prog args.
Attach to an already running process by PID (--attach).
Set an environment variable for the launched program.
Maximum length of printed strings (--string-limit), default 32 - increase it to see passwords in full.
Print paths for file descriptors (--decode-fds).
Show the time spent in each call (--syscall-times).
Timestamps (-t time, -tt with microseconds, -ttt epoch).
Print the call stack after each syscall (--stack-trace).
Summary table: count and time per call instead of the full trace (--summary-only).
Write the trace to a file instead of stderr (--output).
With -o: a separate file per PID (trace.txt.PID).

recipes.note: Full system-call trace - see file opens, network, config reads.
Network calls only, including child processes (where the binary connects to).
Which files the program tries to open (looking for configs/flags).
Call summary - quickly grasp the program's behavior profile.
Library-call trace with long strings - often shows the password comparison via strcmp.
String comparisons only: peek at the entered and expected password in the clear.

tools.notes: The target is a path to an executable file (positional, after the flags), the program arguments follow. LTRACE - same logic, but it intercepts calls into dynamic LIBRARIES (strcmp, memcmp, strcpy, system, getenv) rather than system calls; the flags match: -f children, -e FILTER (malloc+free format), -p PID, -s STRSIZE string length, -o FILE, -c summary, plus -S (also show syscalls), -l LIBRARY (only calls into the specified library). For CTF crackme, ltrace -s is the most useful: the password is often compared via strcmp and is visible right in the trace. They work only with dynamically linked binaries; a static one (ldd says 'not a dynamic executable') will not work with ltrace. Sometimes they require sudo or disabling ptrace protection.
